Dec. 1046, it was held that where an engineer, employed to install machinery on a dredger, is required for the benefit of his employers to live on board, and while preparing his breakfast is injured by the explosion of a gas stove, the injury occurred in the course of the employment.

In Chitty v. Nelson, 126 L. T. J. 172, 2 B. W. C. C. 496, a servant girl, who resided in her employer's home, was suffocated while asleep in her bed through a fire which broke out in the house. It was held by the English courts that the accident arose out of and in the course of her employment.

(e) Recovery has been allowed where employes involuntarily fell asleep at their posts of duty and were injured. Thacker v. McKinney Steel Co., 5 Leading Decisions, W. C. B., p. 165; Sloan v. Thacker Fuel Co. (decided Sept. 21, 1920).

(f) In Adkins v. Sutton Construction Co., 5 Leading Decisions, W. C. B., p. 204, recovery was denied for injuries which an employe sustained by burns, where he made a bed in front of an open fire and voluntarily went to sleep there.

(g) Living on employer's premises as affecting question whether injury to servant arises out of or in the course of the employment. For annotations on this subject, see 31 A. L. R., p. 1251.

(c) The failure to observe the additional duties imposed by subsec. 10 of sec. 331a of the Ky. Stats. (Child Labor Law) as to infant employes, does not constitute an employment of such infant in wilful and known violation of the Child Labor Law, as contemplated by sec. 4911, Ky. Stats. (Workmen's Compensation Act), so as to authorize the guardian of the infant employe, or his representative, if killed, to sue, and thereby waive the benefits of the Compensation Act. Before such election may be made the employment must have been contrary to the terms of the statute, or the infant must have been put to work at some place absolutely prohibited by the statute. Frye's Guardian v. Gamble Brothers, 188 Ky. 283, 221 S. W. 870. (Substance of petition in the above case is stated in the opinion, 188 Ky., p. 285.)

(c) The statute prescribes a specific mode for the acceptance of the provisions of the Act by both employer and employe. In each instance it requires their signatures to the notice required of that fact. Pope Mining Co. v. Brown, 194 Ky. 714, 240 S. W. 755.

(d) It will be presumed that this mode of acceptance is required in order that there may be no uncertainty upon the subject such as would arise if an election were left to the uncertain recollection of witnesses and oftentimes the misunderstood declarations of the parties. Pope Mining Co. v. Brown, 194 Ky. 714, 240 S. W. 755.

(e) The Workmen's Compensation Act of 1916 is an elective and not a compulsory statute, and neither employe nor employer is brought under its provisions until they voluntarily accept them. McCune v. Pell & Brother, 192 Ky. 22, 232 S. W. 43; Greene v. Caldwell, 170 Ky. 571, 186 S. W. 648; Ashland Iron & Mining Co. v. McDaniel, 202 Ky. 22, 258 S. W. 943.

For additional authorities, see American Digest (West Pub. Co.), title Master & Servant, Key No. sec. 358.

(f) To make an injury compensable under the Workmen's Compensation Act, both the employer and the employe must have elected to operate under its provisions, in view of Ky. Stats., secs. 4882, 4956 and 4957, and the employer's acceptance must have been registered as required by law, a condition that cannot be waived by the employe. Bannon v. Watson, 207 Ky. 23, 268 S. W. 573. (See also secs. 64 and 67 of this book.)

(g) Form of employer's notice of acceptance of the Act, see Form 1 in Appendix, under title of Forms.

(h) The signing by an employe of the notice of election prescribed by Workmen's Compensation Act, Ky. Stats., sec. 4957, is not a mere offer to accept a remedy, but is an actual acceptance constituting a contract binding on the employe as well as on the employer electing under section 4956, until withdrawn in the mode prescribed by section 4959. Lee v. Licking Valley Coal Digger Co., 5 Leading Decisions, W. C. B., p. 135.

While we assume that this court is interested only in applying the statute as it is written, we trust that it will not be out of place for us here to say that, from our personal experience in administering this Act as a member of the Board, and being closely in touch with its administration by subsequent members, we are convinced that the Kentucky Act's treatment of the very delicate industrial question of disease as related to industrial injuries is the fairest and most sensible disposition of that problem which has yet been devised in a Workmen's Compensation Law. Under it, in our State, there is no friction between employers and labor growing out of physical examinations for employment, the diseased workman has not been legislated out of a chance to support himself and his family on equal terms with his more fortunate competitors, and if he gets hurt he still receives the maximum compensation for the industrial injury which would be allowable to a normal workman. In fixing this apportionment the Board properly resolves all doubts in favor of the employe and excludes from the compensable period a portion of the injury held to be the result of pre-existing disease only where, and to the extent, that the proof conclusively justifies them.

If any individuals are now desirous of terminating this arrangement, it would seem more advisable for them to present their proposal to the Legislature convening a few months hence, where the accredited and fully informed representatives of the employes and employers will be given due hearing and the full merits of the question gone into in due course, than here to ask the Court of Appeals to take the responsibility of judicially amending the law by striking therefrom this provision.

Such a proposal is particularly serious just at this time, because under a recently adopted construction of section 18 of the Act, relative to permanent partial impairments, and which was upheld in Workmen's Compensation Board, etc., v. U. S. Coal & Coke Co., 196 Ky. 833, 245 S. W. 900, the aggregate cost of compensation under the Kentucky Law has been so increased

The following persons shall be presumed to be wholly dependent upon a deceased employe: (a) A wife upon a husband whom she has not voluntarily abandoned at the time of the accident; (b) A husband incapacitated from wage earning, upon a wife whom he has not voluntarily abandoned at the time of the accident to the wife; (c) A child or children under the age of sixteen years, or over sixteen years if incapacitated from wage earning, upon the parent with whom such child or children are living or by whom actually supported at the time of the accident. In all other cases the relation of dependency in whole or in part shall be determined in accordance with the facts of each case existing at the time of the accident, but no person shall be considered a dependent in any degree unless he be living in the household of the employe at the time of the accident, or unless such person bears to the employe the relation of father, mother, husband or wife, father-in-law or mother-in-law, grandfather or grandmother, child or grandchild, or brother or sister of the whole or half blood. Compensation to any dependent shall cease at the death or legal or common-law marriage of such dependent and upon the cessation of compensation to or on account of any person, the compensation of the remaining persons entitled to compensation shall, for the unexpired period during which their compensation is payable, be that which such persons would have received during such unexpired period if they had been the only persons entitled to compensation at the time of the accident. ## HOW TO ACCEPT THE ACT An employer desiring to accept the Act should fill out a Notice of Acceptance, upon Form No. 1, and mail it to the Board at Frankfort, Ky. It is necessary to state in this notice whether the employer will insure his compensation liability or will apply to carry his own risk. If the carrying of own risk is contemplated, attention is directed to pages 22 and 23 of this Hand Book (pages 780, 781, infra), containing further instruction on that subject. Form No. 2, the Employer's Application to carry own risk, will be mailed to employers designating that method in their Notice of Acceptance. In the above case the court said: "Did the accident which caused the death of Hollenbach arise out of the employment? He was on the master's premises engaged, while washing himself, preparatory to going home. That was a reasonable incident of the employment and he was pursuing it in the usual and ordinary way. The washbasin had been provided by the master for the use of the servants in the basement. The employes were expected to use the basin for lavatory purposes each day. The master also employed the electric current in his business and it was a necessary incident thereof. Electricity is an unfathomed and dangerous instrumentality. It was on the premises, a part of the business, and one of the inherent dangers thereof. Was it not the source and cause of the death of Hollenbach? We are unable to see why his injury and death were not accidents arising out of and in the course of the employment, for the producing cause was so connected with and related to the business of the master as to be an inseparable part of it."

**SEC. 34. BAD AIR, POISONOUS GASES AND FUMES.**

(a) Under the Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act as it existed prior to 1924 it was held by the Court of Appeals in several cases, cited below, that injuries or death caused by the breathing of bad air and poisonous gases were not compensable under the Act. The Legislature of 1924 amended section 1 of the Act, so as to provide that personal injury by accident "shall include injuries or death due to the inhalation in mines of noxious gases or smoke, commonly known as 'bad air'; and also shall include the injuries or death due to the inhalation of any kind of gas."

(b) So that the cases of Jellico Coal Co. v. Adkins, 197 Ky. 684, 247 S. W. 972; Elkhorn Coal Corporation v. Kerr, 203 Ky. 804, 263 S. W. 342; Midland Coal Co. v. Rucker's Admr., 211 Ky. 582, 277 S. W. 838 are no longer good authority. Opinions of the Board to the same effect are White v. Kentucky Collieries

no dependents the employer must also pay the reasonable burial expenses not to exceed $75.00 in any event.

(c) Medical expenses and $100.00. In addition to the burial expenses, if medical expenses are incurred at the instance of the deceased after the employer had failed or refused to provide them (Act sec. 4), the employer would be required to pay to the personal representative a reasonable sum on this account, not to exceed $200.

But besides burial expenses and possible medical expenses, the employer must pay a fixed sum of $100.00 to the personal representative of an employe without dependents (Act sec. 12, subsec. 1), who died from injuries covered by the Act. The provision for paying this sum to the personal representative in any event was probably inserted because of the language of the court in Ky. State Journal Co. v. Workmen's Compensation Board, 161 Ky. 562, 170 S. W. 1166, L. R. A. 1916 A, 389, when the court, in construing section 241 of the Constitution of the State of Kentucky, said: "And it is immaterial, under this section of the Constitution, whether the money recovered goes to the children or parents, or becomes part of his personal estate. The disposition of the money after his death cannot affect the right of the personal representative to recover. It may go to his heirs, or it may become a part of his personal estate and go to his creditors."

(For definition of "average weekly wages" see sec. 24 of the Act.)

On October 27, 1923, an employe was killed in an accident. In November of that year the employer paid $75.00 burial expense. On November 14, 1923, a motion to reopen the case was filed. On April 1, 1925, an application for adjustment of the claim was filed by the administrator of the employe. It was admitted that the employe died without dependents. The application of the administrator was for payment of $100.00, as provided by section 4893, section 12 of the Act. Section 33 of the Act (sec. 4914 of the Statutes) provides that a claim must be filed within one year after the date of the accident or, in case of death, within one year after such death.

SEC. 391. ACCEPTANCE OF ACT BY EMPLOYE—GENERALLY.

(a) After the employer has elected to accept the Act, the employe must be given notice of this fact and an opportunity to decide whether to agree with his employer to operate under this law. The agreement with the employer must be in the form prescribed in section 74 of the Act. The date on which the agreement is signed must be set opposite the name of the employe, and the Act is operative from this date. Any number of employes may sign the same agreement.

(b) The acceptance of the Act thus made will remain effective, so long as the employe remains in the employment of the employer with whom he agreed, or until it is withdrawn under section 76 of the Act. But if there are suspensions of the employment for less than a year the original acceptance will continue effective in a subsequent employment by the same employer, unless withdrawn in the manner provided under section 76 of the Act. SEC. 264. WHEN EMPLOYER IS "MISLED TO HIS INJURY."

(a) Section 36 of the Act provides that notices shall not be held "invalid or insufficient by reason of any inaccuracy in complying with section 34 hereof, unless it shall be shown that the employer was in fact misled to his injury thereby." (a) The courts are in conflict as to whether death by lightning constitutes an accident arising out of and in the course of employment. The better ruling seems to be that death by lightning is not covered by the Act. One of the basic principles upon which Workmen's Compensation Acts are founded is that the workingman needs protection from the natural hazards of the business in which he is engaged other than that afforded by the common law. The danger of death or injury by lightning is common to all classes of people. While a stroke of lightning is within the definition of the word "accident" and may be suffered "in the course of employment" it seems that the meaning of the words must be strained to say that it "arose out of" the employment. As that law very materially increased the defenses of the employer against an employe who rejected the Act, the court held that this rendered his waiver of his right to an unlimited recovery under section 54 involuntary, and therefore invalid. It will be necessary, on this account, for any future law to leave the employe, in the event of a refusal to accept the Act, with the same cause of action and subject only to the same defenses in a suit at law as now exist. Practically all the other elective acts recognized this situation, even where not drawn under sections like our section 54, and provided that where the employer rejects the Act he becomes suable at law with the common-law defenses removed, but that where he accepts the Act and the employe rejects it, all these defenses are restored to the employer in the event of suit. On superficial inspection and in view of the claims of a class of interested third parties that a Compensation Law is very unfair to the employe, one might suppose that few employes would accept such a law in preference to their present right of suit for damages. The statistics of all the fifteen states which have tried elective laws show, however, that the percentage of employes who do not accept under these conditions is negligible. In Massachusetts, for example, their law afforded but a 50 per cent rate of compensation and comparatively low maximum benefits. Of the 650,000 employes whose employers accepted that Act—considerably the largest number of employes who have ever been brought under one elective law—but 490 failed to accept it, less than one-tenth of one per cent. It may be assumed that they will just as uniformly accept a law affording a 65 per cent rate of compensation and more liberal maximum benefits, as is here recommended for Kentucky. ## ORGANIZATION OF THE VOLUNTARY COMMISSION Following the extension of the opinion of the Court of Appeals a movement, originating in the Attorney General's Department, was instituted looking to the formation of a non- 731 # INDEX

SEC. 129. (SEC. 13 OF THE ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4894.) DEPENDENTS DEFINED. The following persons shall be presumed to be wholly dependent upon a deceased employe: (a) A wife upon a husband whom she has not voluntarily abandoned at the time of the accident; (b) A husband incapacitated from wage earning, upon a wife whom he has not voluntarily abandoned at the time of the accident to the wife; (c) A child or children under the age of sixteen years, or over sixteen years if incapacitated from wage earning, upon the parent with whom such child or children are living or by whom actually supported at the time of the accident. In all other cases the relation of dependency in whole or in part shall be determined in accordance with the facts of each case existing at the time of the accident, but no person shall be considered a dependent in any degree unless he be living in the household of the employe at the time of the accident, or unless such person bears to the employe the relation of father, mother, husband or wife, father-in-law or mother-in-law, grandfather or grandmother, child or grandchild, or brother or sister of the whole or half blood.

Compensation to any dependent shall cease at the death or legal or common-law marriage of such dependent and upon the cessation of compensation to or on account of any person, the compensation of the remaining persons entitled to compensation shall, for the unexpired period during which their compensation is payable, be that which such persons would have received during such unexpired period if they had been the only persons entitled to compensation at the time of the accident. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 13, p. 363.)

(g) In the case of Johnson v. Johnson, 145 Ky. 488, 140 S. W. 656, an action by a wife against her husband for divorce on the ground of abandonment, it was held that though the actual abandonment was by the wife, she might nevertheless procure a divorce from her husband on that ground where it appears "that by his conduct or treatment he creates conditions that prevent the wife from living with him in decency, comfort and peace." Of course this rule would apply to an analogous case brought by the husband on the same ground. It is seen therefore that in actions for divorce an abandonment may exist in favor of the party to the marriage relation who is nevertheless the one who has physically abandoned the other.

Applying that rule to the case of a wife who after abandonment by her husband attempts to procure and in good faith thinks she has procured a divorce from him, and who in good faith remarries, we think that conduct on her part a voluntary abandonment by her of him, notwithstanding his abandonment of her in the first instance and the invalidity of her subsequent marriage. Whenever she voluntarily puts or attempts to put it out of her power to legally resume the marital relation with him, and to forfeit her claim upon him for support, then we think that conduct a voluntary abandonment by her, notwithstanding what their previous relations may have been. Ison v. R. T. Davis Coal Co., 4 Leading Decisions, W. C. B., p. 52.

SEC. 161. EFFECT OF DEATH OR MARRIAGE OF DEPENDENT.

(a) Section 13 of the Act provides: "Compensation to any dependent shall cease at the death or legal or common-law marriage of such dependent, and upon the cessation of compensation to or on account of any persons, the compensation of the remaining persons entitled to compensation shall, for the unexpired period during which their compensation is payable, be that which such persons would have received during such unexpired period if they had been the only persons entitled to compensation at the time of the accident."
